figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Previous methods [13, 19, 45, 46, 52] fail to adequately model LiDAR-specific properties, suffer from entangled dynamic-static features, and must rely on ground truth ego status, limiting their ability to understand the driving world. (b) In contrast, our approach explicitly disentangles dynamic and static elements following LiDAR’s physical structure. This enables more accurate scene comprehension while supporting autonomous rollout and reliable “what-if” reasoning.
Figure 5. Visual comparisons with competitive LiDAR world models on nuScenes [3] and KITTI Odometry [7] datasets.
Table 1. Quantitative comparisons of world modeling accuracy with other state-of-the-art methods on nuScenes [3].
Table 2. Quantitative comparisons of world modeling accuracy with other state-of-the-art methods on KITTI Odometry [7].
Table 3. Quantitative comparisons of generated distribution realism with other state-of-the-art methods on three datasets.

Figure 2. Our LiDAR scene tokenizer first projects the point cloud P into a dense range map R. A scan-based Mamba encoder then process the map into latent features z, which are discretized into ẑ via a vector quantization codebook. After this, a decoder is used to perform the upsampling and reconstruct ẑ into output R̂, which is finally converted back into a point cloud P̂. To enhance the reconstruction quality, particularly for range maps dominated by low-frequency signals, a discriminator is incorporated into our training process.
Figure 6. The capability of GEM to perform autonomous rollout (AR) and future prediction. GEM (AR) uses an integrated planner to provide future ego status, instead of ground truth.
Figure 7. The ability of GEM to perform controllable generation and counterfactual reasoning, where royal blue dots represent the ego vehicle, and dots in other colors represent other vehicles.

Figure 3. The process of feature disentanglement and aggregation in GEM. First, dynamic-static separator separates the latent features in an unsupervised manner into three components: dynamic features Fd, static features Fs, and generic features F . The tri-path deformable Mamba then adaptively generates specialized scan paths {pg,pd,ps} for each branches, which guide the sampling of intermediate features {F̄g, F̄d, F̄s} via bilinear interpolation. These features are then passed by Mamba within their respective branch, obtaining {F ′ g,F ′ d,F ′ s}. Finally, an adaptive gated attention fuses these features to produce the refined feature Fg for future frame generation.
